title: 【5割が“クソ仕事”で人生を終える】「才能のムダが世界を壊す」歴史家ルトガー・ブレグマン／日本に必要なのは“同調圧力からの自立”／「高潔な敗者」にならず→3.5％で未来を変えろ【1on1】

transcript.text: [music]
[music]
[music]
>> So Rutger Bregman, welcome to our show.
Thanks for having me. Great to be with
you. Yes, so I just read your wonderful
book Moral Ambition. Are you excited
about the Japanese edition? [music]
>> I am extremely excited about it. Um, my
Japanese readers have always been
amazing ever since my very first book
Utopia for Realists.
I love visiting Japan back in 2017. So,
I can't wait to
yeah,
hear what Japanese readers think of this
one because it builds on all my previous
books. I guess so because I think the
Japanese people [music] love to talk
moral, but it's interesting that you
combine moral [music] with ambition. So,
I'd like to talk to you about this
today.
Sure, it's somewhat of an unusual
combination, but I think it's an
incredibly important thing we need right
now.
Um, so moral ambition is pretty simple
actually. It is
the combination of the idealism of let's
say an activist and the ambition of an
entrepreneur. It is the desire to make
this world a wildly better place.
Um, to use what you have, your time,
your talent, your resources to make the
biggest possible difference. Interesting
thing. So, you should be not only a good
person, but also a strategist. I talked
to 2 years ago and I think the world has
become
unfortunately crazier than before.
What's happening in Iran? We haven't
solved climate crisis yet. So, we have
these smart people, we have artificial
intelligence, all the technology, but
why is the world not becoming a better
place? I think we are living at a hinge
point in history. I think that the next
5 to 10 years are going to be some of
the wildest years in all of human
history. And I am really hesitant to say
this because I'm trained as a historian.
I'm I'm very aware that people often
suffer from something called
chronocentrism, right? People often
think that the time they happen to be
alive is the most important moment. That
is
often not true.
But this time is really different and it
is different because of two reasons. The
first one is the decay of the
international global order. We see it
collapsing all around. US hegemony,
you know, the notion that we had a
rules-based international order. Um, we
just see the the rise of autocracy
everywhere. And in the US, I would even
call it a form of neo-fascism.
Um, and then at the same time, we are in
the midst of an extraordinary profound
technological revolution. And I still
think that a lot of people
don't realize how profound it really is.
I think that often when people, you
know, hear the big promises of Silicon
Valley, they're like maybe it is like
blockchain, maybe it is like the NFTs,
maybe it is like the metaverse, you
know, it will be another one of those
big stories, another one of those
bubbles that will collapse.
But no, AI is really different. I think
it is going to change everything. It is
already changing everything. I have
multiple
wow moments every week right now. But I
mean, this is just this is just the
beginning. The questions that AI raises
are are going to be much more profound.
Uh, the whole social contract will have
to be rewritten. Um, the way things work
right now where people work for their
money, they pay taxes, that's their
contribution, that's how they
participate in the social contract, then
they, you know, have the right to vote,
can choose their own leaders.
All of that is under threat right now.
Because we used to have a situation with
sovereign nations, then we went to a
situation with sovereign companies, and
now we're moving towards a situation
with sovereign individuals where just
the CEOs of the tech companies
accumulate so much power
that the question is really
can democracy survive in in an age like
this? I agree cuz I think there's a
power shift from nation-state to an
individual or maybe a individual with
AI. So, let's let's let's go back to
your point. You made two points which is
very interesting that number one, the
world is collapsing, it's a mess. And
number two, we have this awesome
technology. So, why are we not using
this awesome technology to empower
ourselves to fix those problems?
It's a big question. A couple of things.
So, often when I look at people on,
let's say, the left-wing progressive
side of the political spectrum,
I kind of want to scream at them.
I think a lot of them have their head in
the sand and they have no idea what's
coming. A lot of my friends, academics,
pundits, they're still uh, thinking that
oh, this is just advanced auto-complete.
It's just a stochastic parrot as they
say. Um, they don't realize what it is
already able to do and what it will be
able to do in the future. So, that is
that is one reason. The other reason is
that um,
the dystopian potential of this
technology is so vast. Look, I have
spent pretty much my whole career
thinking about how we can make this
world a wildly better place. Yes. Right?
My very first book, Utopia for Realists.
I remember very well when I first came
to Japan and had the honor, you know, to
talk to Japanese readers about that
book. It was about ideas like a
universal basic income.
>> Yes. Right? So, that when the robots
would do the work, we would all get the
the the the income so that we can
actually live our lives. Um,
I am still very much in favor of such
ambitious plans. It's just that it is
very late right now and
I'm kind of sad, but not not a lot of
people have been listening to me in the
last [laughter] 10 years. I mean, that
book was already full of I mean, again
and again I was emphasizing this is
coming, this is coming, people. We got
to prepare ourselves. But now it's
pretty late. Um, and um, I think that
we're just in a really bad situation
where the the the accumulation of power
is enormous, the race is speeding up,
and the risks are immense. So, one of
the most important things to do right
now is to just slow down.
>> [laughter]
>> Honestly, I think that is one of the
most important imperatives right now.
Um, and that's that's what it means to
be morally ambitious in this space right
now. So, how can we slow down?
I think we can do that in a couple of
ways. The analogy that I used in a
recent lecture is the analogy to the
temperance movement. Hm. So, in the 19th
century in the West, you had three big
moral movements.
>> Mhm. The first movement was the fight
against slavery. The second movement was
the right for the fight for women's
rights. And the third one was the
temperance movement. Now, everyone
remembers the first two movements, but
but everyone has forgotten the third.
The temperance movement was a movement
against big alcohol. Ah. So, at the time
in the United States, alcohol was
extremely cheap, huge amounts of men
were extremely addicted, they spent
almost all their money on alcohol, and
then came home and beat up their wives,
and they were terrible dads. Then what
happened is that a movement arose
against that. I call it the first big
addiction industry. You know, just as
people are addicted to social media
today, back then people were addicted to
alcohol. And what happened is that a
huge bipartisan movement, people from
the left to the right, you know, um,
workers to
Orthodox Christians and Evangelicals,
they united in one big movement. And the
main thing they wanted, well, it was to
stop big alcohol. Hm. Uh, it kind of
escalated, I have to say. So,
uh, you probably remember from history
class that at some point they passed
prohibition in the United States. So, it
became completely illegal to even sell
alcohol. Uh, that that was, you know,
taking things a little bit too far in my
opinion.
>> Yeah.
Yeah.
>> Yeah. But it shows you the power of this
kind of movement. And my prediction is
that this is just starting to happen.
So, what you see in poll after poll in
the United States, in Europe, in Japan,
is that people
um,
are scared. And the salience of AI and
AI safety is increasing. So, in the US,
people are already more worried than
about AI than about global warming,
which is a pretty profound thing. I
think the next elections, the midterm
elections, will be the first AI
elections. I think that the presidential
elections in 2028 will mostly be around
AI. So, it is by far the most important
thing that's happening right now and a
lot of people are deeply, deeply
skeptical. And now is the time to to
leverage their power because if we're
too late, you know,
10 years from now,
you know, you might not have that power
anymore. So, how can we use that power
because in your book, you discuss a lot
about talent and allocating talent
because you discuss argue that a lot of
people are not using talent in an
effective way. You know, for instance,
people really smart talented people,
they're going to Silicon Valley creating
social media apps or X or Facebook or
Instagram, which are only making people
addicted. How can we use that talent or
maybe the viewers of this podcast to use
their skills to make the world a better
place? The greatest waste of our time is
the waste of talent. There are millions
and millions of people who are currently
stuck in jobs that don't really make the
world a better place. You know, they're
sometimes described as the BS jobs.
>> People who can go on strike.
Yeah. [laughter]
Uh,
not sure if I was allowed to say that.
>> academic term, so.
>> [laughter]
>> But yeah, and very often these people
have incredible resumes. They went to
some of the best universities. So, take
a place like Harvard University. I was
recently there and you know, you
discover that around 50% of Harvard
graduates end up either in finance or
consultancy or work for big tech. And
very often they think that their own job
is kind of socially meaningless. So,
that is a huge waste because we actually
need these people to tackle some of the
greatest challenges, whether that is you
know, the breakdown of the climate or
the way our democracy is threatened or
the risk of the next pandemic. We need
our best and brightest. We need the most
talented, smartest people to work on the
biggest challenges. So, that's the first
thing I've I've always been obsessed
with. The second thing is the simple
insight that small groups of very
thoughtful, committed citizens can
change the world. So, history is not
just governed by the big laws, you know,
by the big structures. No, it is also
sometimes governed by small groups of
really determined people, for better or
for worse. So, if you look around at the
group in Silicon Valley, you know, the
PayPal mafia, the group around Peter
Thiel, the venture capitalist and and
around Elon Musk. It's a very small
group, but they've been extremely
influential, built these huge companies
from Palantir to Meta to SpaceX.
So, it's an example of how small groups
of really ambitious, dedicated people
can make a massive difference.
And we can also use that power for good
in my view. So, what I do in my work is
that I look at a lot of historical
examples where people were able to do
this, whether that's abolitionism, you
know, the fight against the slave trade
or the rise of social democracy, the
those who build out the welfare state.
Again and again, it is small groups of
really committed people who make the
difference and this book is basically a
rallying cry for those people around the
globe. I'm basically inviting people to
join one of those groups and to stop
being a bystander and actually
join
the fight in the arena.
I totally agree with you because I've
heard of the 3.5% rule if in the world
to change, only 3.5% can start the
change. Do you see that happening or do
you think it's just 0% right now and
people have to start reading your book
and start the change?
I think we are seeing the beginnings of
a shift. So, I think there are a lot of
people right now who walk around
with a hole in their soul, you know, a a
hole where meaning used to be. And for
centuries, religions were able to
provide the scaffolding, you know,
religions were able to provide a sense
of belonging, a sense of meaning, a
sense of purpose. They were able to
answer the the big questions of of human
existence.
Where do we come from? Where do we go?
Why are we here? How should we live?
What is sacred, right? Those are the
most important questions that we humans
have to answer
during our time on this planet and the
old answers don't work anymore. So, a
lot of people are restless. They're
looking for new answers.
And this is partially what we're trying
to do with the organization that I
co-founded called the School for Moral
Ambition that we launched at the same
time as we launched our book. And the
growth of it has just been incredible to
witness. So, in just 2 years, we we got
25,000 members from more than 140
countries. Hundreds in in Japan as well
and I think that'll only grow as the
book comes out there. And all these
people
yeah, they're they're they're looking
for something more. They don't just want
to talk the talk, they want to walk the
walk. They don't want to be bystanders
and look on as, you know, things
deteriorate and deteriorate. They want
to take their own responsibility. And
that is obviously very encouraging to
see. It's just that we need much more of
it.
So, let's go on to the second part of
this show. Ideals need strategy. So, we
talked about your title, such an
interesting title because moral
ambitions. So, for me or even Japanese
leaders, moral means becoming like a
perfect person. You have to be a very
good person. Is that what you're stating
or do you think it's not just being a
good person, but also a person with
realistic strategy, you know?
Can you elaborate on that? So, in the
fight against injustice, whether that is
the exploitation of people or of
animals,
what really matters is that you are
actively actually effective, that you
actually make a difference. You really
don't want to be what I call the noble
losers. You know, noble losers are those
people who
you know, may happen to be right, they
may happen to be correct, but they don't
actually win.
And that's why one of the chapters in
this book is called winning is a moral
duty, right?
The the reason we remember people like
Martin Luther King or Rosa Parks is not
just because they were courageous,
it's also because they won, right?
Because they were able to push four huge
packages of legislation through American
Congress and you know, won a decisive
decisive battle in the fight against
American segregation. Um,
if I look at a lot of progressive
movements today and progressive
movements in the last 10 years,
I see a lot of noble losers if I'm
honest. I see a lot of people who were
very happy to stand on the right side of
history, but who didn't actually achieve
much. So, I'll give you an famous or
infamous example from the United States.
You'll remember this, the Black Lives
Matter movement. It was the biggest
movement in the history of the country.
Um, tens of millions of people joined,
but they pretty much achieved
nothing, right? There's no major
legislation that came out of that. And
actually you could argue that there was
a big backlash against it and the whole
rise of Trump is can partially be
explained by what was what is sometimes
called the great awakening.
You know, all these people becoming very
woke. If you are morally serious,
you're obsessed with actually achieving
results.
So, is it because, you know, Black Lives
Matter, there was social media and
people are just posting comments on
social media instead of Rosa Parks, she
went on the bus or Martin Luther King
Jr. he did a speech, he did a march. Is
it the difference between social media
and real actions or is there something
different between the two events? Yes.
So, one of the illusions that noble
losers have is they think that awareness
is
is very important. I always like to say
that awareness is very overrated. So, a
lot people know about the great problems
that we face as a species. I'll give you
one of my favorite examples. A lot of
people know that the way we treat
animals
is really bad, right? A lot of people
who do even 10 minutes of research into
factory farms and the way we treat pigs
and the way we treat chickens, they're
like, "Ooh, this is this is really bad."
It's just that they choose to ignore it.
They choose not to think about it
because it is kind of uncomfortable to
think about it. Because if you really
think about it, then you realize you got
to do something about it and action is
is hard. In this social media age, it is
very easy to post something on
Instagram, post something on Facebook or
X or whatever social medium you you you
prefer.
And to have the feeling that you made a
difference, right? But that is that is
just a first step maybe.
>> [laughter]
>> What really matters is actually doing
something about all these things. And if
you if we look at the movements of, say,
half a century ago,
they didn't have social media,
obviously. So, they needed to build a
very big infrastructure to organize
something like a march on Washington.
And that meant that people had a lot of
face-to-face contact, right? There there
was a lot of work to do to
actually gather all these people.
But today, you can you can send out a
message on social media and then, you
know, millions of people could be there
for your big protest next the next day,
but then it also very quickly disappears
because there's nothing connecting all
these people, right? There is no
infrastructure. And building the the
infrastructure is hard. That just takes
a lot of work.
Do you think we need time or
face-to-face communication to build that
infrastructure? That is something
important, crucial.
>> Yeah.
Absolutely. So, we talked about my
obsession with uh you know, small groups
that change the course of history. I've
always been really interested in those
groups on the right-wing side of the
political spectrum. So, if I can give a
few examples from the US again, um the
Federalist Society was a group that
started in the 1980s and they pumped the
whole judicial system full of their
people. They knew it was going to take
years and years and years, but
eventually many of these people ended up
on the Supreme Court. And that is
partially how they were able to destroy
Roe v. Wade, which was the federal
um
you know, right to abortion. Um it was a
decades-long battle. But
even though I disagree with their
political goals, I admire that
perseverance. And then if I look at the
other side of the political spectrum, I
don't see that perseverance. I don't see
that institution building. I don't see
many philanthropists, for example, or
donors who don't think in election
cycles, but think in generations. Um
I think we need much more long-term
thinking there and long-term ambition.
To realize that we are part of something
way, way bigger. Um so,
my two favorite examples are on the
other side are always, you know, the
fight against the slave trade and the
fight for women's rights. Well,
the most successful abolitionist
movement was was in Britain actually, in
the United Kingdom. And of the 12
founders of the British Society for the
abolition of the slave trade, which was
founded in 1787,
only one was still alive when slavery
was finally abolished across the British
Empire. So, that is what it takes, you
know, you need an enormous amount of
perseverance and the odds are pretty
high that you won't even be around when
your team is finally victorious.
So, you know, people hate liberals, the
media, everywhere in the world. Do you
think
we you know, the media or liberals or
they're not trying hard enough. They're
just talking and not trying hard enough
to build that organization. What is
happening now is that something that we
took for granted for many years, the
post-war liberal social democratic order
is increasingly becoming the new
resistance. So,
after the Second World War, you know,
after the enormous destruction around
the globe, after the terrible atrocities
that were committed, um people came
together and they crafted this document
called the Universal Declaration of
Human Rights.
And
so many nations around the globe signed
it. It was an extraordinary moment
because this document was extremely
utopian, right? Um it had all these
aspirations about what we would do for
people as individuals and what we would
do to guarantee their rights.
But it what happened is that over the
decades, we kind of became lazy.
Especially after the fall of the Berlin
Wall, people started to think that, you
know, they had arrived at the end of
history.
>> Yes. And that as long as economic growth
would continue, that slowly all other
countries would become more democratic
as well. That China uh you know, would
become more democratic uh because once
they would have, I don't know, like
smartphones and social media, surely
they would realize that there's only one
way and that is the American way.
Well, that was a complete and total
illusion. I think Mark Carney, the Prime
Minister of Canada, said it really well
at the World Economic Forum earlier this
year in Davos. Um
this era is over and it is not coming
back. Um those who still believe in the
values of liberal democracy, countries
like Canada,
um continents like Europe or the
European Union, countries like Japan, we
are the new resistance. We're not the
most powerful,
sadly. Uh we're not China, we're not the
US. We are being squeezed by the great
powers and what we need to do right now
is to be dead serious about what we
actually think. We need to band
together. We need to improve our
leverage. We need to build our
militaries. We need to strengthen our
countries. All kinds of things that were
unthinkable for a long time should be
thinkable right now. I was yesterday I
was talking to Die Zeit, uh you know,
the big German newspaper. And I said,
"Germany should really consider building
nuclear weapons right now." That's
something I never would have said, you
know, 10 years ago. But I think that's
absolutely crucial right now. The free
world should be able to defend itself.
It should be able to withstand pressure
from the bullies around the globe. And
we cannot rely on America anymore. We
have been betrayed by the US and now we
need to rely on ourselves. It's
interesting because nuclear weapons are
also a sensitive topic in Japan and you
know, the media and the liberals are
very it's difficult to discuss, but we
need we can't be lazy. We need to
challenge ourselves and think Yes.
>> Yes. From scratch.
Yes. And I completely understand,
obviously, that especially in Japan,
this is an extremely sensitive topic. Um
but we also need the self-confidence,
right? In a in a in a world
You see it in with the war in Iran,
right? What does that war even purpose
does it even serve, right? It is just
the authoritarian neo-fascist dynamic
where the president of the United States
is like, "Well, I just want to do that."
We can't rely on on countries like that
as our partners anymore. And and Japan
lives under that umbrella, obviously,
for for a long time, but it is gone. We
need to
wake up. And um like I would I would
also as a Dutchman, I would much rather
trust on, you know, a dependency of
Japan, you know, a country that has an
incredible tradition of after the Second
World War of governance, of democracy.
Like
>> [laughter]
>> we should we should hold each other very
closely because uh it's getting dark out
there. Winter is coming and uh we all
need to wake up.
Thank you. Let's go to the final part.
Um how can we become a person with moral
ambitions? So, your book and the talk
today personally struck me because when
I last talked to you, I was in a startup
and I pivot my career. Right now, I'm at
TBS, a very huge uh national TV
organization. I'm trying to change this
company.
>> [clears throat]
>> I want to make this company digital. So,
I'm saying every day that we should go
digital, but people think that I'm
right, but it takes time to build
institution and change something. So,
could you give me advice or our viewers
advice?
>> [laughter and gasps]
>> How we can be idealistic, but at the
same time being real, being realistic
and being a strategist? I will do the
will be the last one to deny that it's
really hard to change a big media
organization from the inside.
>> [laughter]
>> That is really hard.
>> Okay. Okay. So, I would say what you
need is a startup within a big
organization like that. You need to
gather your own small cult, if you will,
of really committed, really ambitious,
really dedicated people who will just
start something inside that that Moloch.
Um and then hopefully it can spread from
there. Look, I don't think there's like
one way to change the world, obviously
not. Like sometimes you you just need to
start from scratch because the old
models are broken. But sometimes
entrepreneurship can actually work,
right? Sometimes people are effective at
changing big corporations or big
organizations from within. It's really
hard, but some people have been
successful at it. Um
moral ambition is just about using what
you have, right? Uh whether that's your
time or your talent or or your financial
capital to to make a very big
difference, to be
kind of I like to call it morally
greedy, right?
>> [laughter]
>> Some people may say, "Look, that's not
for me. I'm just an ordinary human
being." But if you study the
biographies, if you read the memoirs of
the great figures of history, you
actually realize
they were just like us. They're ordinary
people. Uh moral ambition is not some
kind of thing you are born with. It is
actually an idea. It's a contagious
idea, right? And you can get infected
with it. So, do you think in a way we're
not ambitious enough because instead of
building cathedrals or starting a civil
rights movement, we're only building
those tiny apps? Yes. And here's where
the Netherlands, where I'm from, and
Japan have something in common, right? I
think both our countries, both our
cultures suffer from what is sometimes
called tall poppy syndrome. Or in the
Netherlands, we call it the the the
hayfield culture. As soon as you stick
your head out, you know, uh above the
hayfield, we chop it off. Um it's hard,
culturally hard to be ambitious in both
the Netherlands and Japan. And that is I
think one of the reasons why, for
example, all the big tech companies are
in the US. I recently lived in the US
for a year and there's many things you
can say about what's wrong with that
country, but they do have a culture of
ambition, right? They do have a culture
of dreaming big. And I think we need
more and more of that attitude. You
could say that in in in America, it has
become illegal to be moral, but in
Europe and Japan, it's often illegal to
be ambitious.
>> [laughter]
>> So, we should give people the social
license to dream bigger, to have much
more ambition, because that that
ambition is such precious creative
energy that we should try and channel in
the right direction.
So, moral and ambition, what's the best
mix? 50% 50% or what's
your advice?
I would say 100% and 100%.
>> [laughter]
>> Yeah. No, look, I I'm saying that
life is about many things, right? So,
I'm not saying that being morally
ambitious is the only way to live a good
life. There are many ways to live a good
life. I'm also not saying that your
moral ambition should suck up
everything.
I am a dad. I've got a 4-year-old. I've
got a 1-year-old. Being a good dad is
extremely important to me. Being a good
husband is extremely important to me.
But, I will also say that there are
trade-offs. Um let me tell you the story
of my favorite hero in all of world
history. This was a young man named
Thomas Clarkson. He was a student at
Cambridge University in the United
Kingdom. And when he was 25, he
participated in an essay contest. And by
chance, the question that the students
had to answer was, is it okay? Is it
morally defensible to own and sell other
human beings? Is Is slavery okay?
And he had never really thought about
the question. But, then he started
writing the essay and he realized, well,
it's actually really bad. Um he wrote
the whole thing, won first prize, and
then there was this famous moment. In
the summer of 1785, he was on his horse
from Cambridge where he would just
receive the award to London where he
lived, and he was near the village of
Wadesmill, a small tiny village. Um and
he kept thinking about what he had just
written, and then he realized,
"But, wait a minute. If this is true, if
